title: Building a three-bin hot compost system
subtitle: Wooden-pallet build, with a finished bin in eight to twelve weeks
excerpt: A three-bin hot compost system built from four wooden pallets, hinges, and a handful of screws. Each bin holds 1 m³, enough to reach the 55 to 65°C core temperature that pasteurises weed seeds and finishes compost in eight to twelve weeks rather than a year. Weekend build, multi-month cycle.
sourceNotes: Garden Organic compost-system guidance for the three-bin sequence and the C:N ratio target. RHS Composting fact sheet for the 55 to 65°C hot-pile temperature range. ADAS / WRAP small-scale composting trials for the eight-to-twelve-week hot-pile finish time and the moisture-management cues. Build pattern adapted from the common UK gardening-allotment tradition (pre-existing public domain craft); pallet selection rule (HT-stamp only, never MB) from UK Forestry Commission ISPM-15 guidance.

content.text: A three-bin compost system is the working standard for a household producing more than a single dustbin's worth of garden and kitchen waste a year. Three 1 m³ cubes side by side: bin 1 holds the active fresh-fill heap, bin 2 receives the first turn at two weeks, bin 3 receives the second turn at four weeks and yields finished compost at eight to twelve weeks. The cubic-metre scale matters, anything smaller cannot hold the thermal mass needed for
hot composting
. A 60 cm cube cold-composts; a 1 m cube hot-composts.

This tutorial builds the system from four wooden pallets per bin, twelve pallets total. The pallets are scrounged free from builders' merchants, agricultural suppliers, or industrial estates. The hinges, screws, and removable front-slat hardware are the only paid kit. The build is a weekend job for two people. The composting cycle is the long arc that follows.

attrs.body: A 1 m³ hot-compost bin processes roughly 6 m³ of garden and kitchen waste per year. The volume halves, then halves again, as the material breaks down. Each bin yields about 1 m³ of finished compost per year, enough to topdress a 50 m² vegetable plot at the recommended 20 mm depth. Where the council charges for garden waste collection, the bin recoups its cost in the first year. Where the council collects free, the saving is in not buying bagged compost. 1000 litres of finished home compost displaces a lot of bagged purchases over a season. A well-built pallet bin lasts 5 to 7 years before the bottom rails rot through. Replacing a single pallet is a half-hour job.
attrs.title: What this saves

content.text: What you need, materials
Twelve wooden pallets, 1200 × 1000 mm
(the standard UK / EUR pallet footprint). Four pallets per bin: back, two sides, base. Each pallet must carry the IPPC mark and the letters
HT

(heat-treated). Never use pallets stamped MB, methyl-bromide fumigation is no longer permitted in UK / EU pallets but historic stock still circulates, and the chemical leaches into compost. Unstamped pallets of unknown origin are a no; rotten or oil-stained pallets are a no.
Six T-hinges, 100 mm
. Two per joining edge for the front panels. Galvanised; stainless if the bin sits in a wet position.
Approximately 80 wood screws, 4.0 × 50 mm
for joining the pallets, plus shorter 4.0 × 30 mm screws for the hinges. Quantity scales, buy a tub of 100 of each and use what's needed.
Six lengths of 50 × 25 mm tanalised batten, 1100 mm long

. Two per bin, screwed vertically inside the front-pallet uprights to form the channels the removable front slats slide into. Tanalised softwood (pressure-treated against rot) lasts 5+ years in ground contact.
Approximately 18 removable front slats
, six per bin, cut from pallet boards or 100 × 22 mm gravel board, each 1000 mm long. The slats stack horizontally to close the front; lift them out one at a time to access the heap.
One roll of corrugated cardboard or three feed sacks
for the top cover. The cover keeps rain off (compost should be moist, not soaked) and traps heat. Old hessian or carpet underlay works; modern carpet does not, synthetic fibres shed microplastics.
What you need, tools

Cordless drill with a Pozidrive bit, handsaw, tape measure, spirit level, pencil. A square helps for marking square cuts but isn't essential. Once the bins are running you'll also need the compost thermometer for monitoring and a long-handled garden fork for the turn.
Site the bins

Pick the site before you build. The three bins occupy 3 m of run along one wall, plus 300 mm clearance behind for ventilation. Choose level ground; full sun is fine in the UK climate (a hot heap dries faster but reaches a higher peak temperature), part-shade is fine too. The bins want close access to the kitchen door, the further the walk, the less the bin gets used. A spot 10 to 15 m from the back door is typical; 30 m starts to feel inconvenient. Site at least 5 m from any windows; a well-managed hot heap does not smell, but a slow / wet / out-of-balance heap can.

Direct contact with soil is essential, the bins sit on bare earth, not concrete or paving slabs. Worms migrate up into the heap from the soil; soil bacteria seed the pile. If your only available site is paved, lay 200 mm of compost or topsoil under the bins to give the biology a foothold.
The build
1. Prepare the pallets

Inspect every pallet. Confirm the IPPC heat-treatment stamp. Pull out any protruding nails with a claw hammer; trim any split or rotten boards with the handsaw. If a pallet has a deeply broken stringer (the load-bearing 70 × 100 mm runners), reject it, the build relies on the stringers carrying the joined-edge screws.
2. Build the first bin

Stand the back pallet vertically, deck facing inward (so the slats are vertical when you look at the back of the bin from the back of the garden). Stand a side pallet vertically and butt it perpendicular to the back pallet at one end. Drill three 3 mm pilot holes through the stringer of the side pallet into the corner stringer of the back pallet, top, middle, and bottom. Drive a 4.0 × 50 mm screw through each pilot hole. Check square with the spirit level held diagonally, the bin should be square enough that the front slats will slide cleanly later.

Repeat for the second side pallet. The fourth pallet, the base, lays flat on the soil inside the U-shape, deck side up. Screw the base to the bottom rails of the back and side pallets with another twelve screws. The base raises the heap 100 mm off the soil, improving drainage; some builders skip the base entirely and let the heap sit on bare earth, which is also fine.
3. Add the front-slat channels

Take two 1100 mm lengths of 50 × 25 mm tanalised batten. Screw each vertically to the inside face of the front edge of a side pallet, set back 25 mm from the outer face so a 25 mm front slat can sit flush with the pallet's outer face. The two battens face each other across the front opening, creating a vertical channel on either side. The front slats will stack horizontally into these channels, and lift out one at a time when you need to access the heap.

Cut the front slats to length (1000 mm, or measure the gap between battens and subtract 5 mm for sliding clearance) and stack them in. The full stack closes the front to the same height as the side pallets; the top stays open for the cardboard cover.
4. Build bins 2 and 3

Repeat the build twice more, butting each new bin's side pallet to the previous bin's outer side pallet, sharing the joint. The whole three-bin run sits as one rigid 3000 × 1000 mm structure; the shared walls reduce the pallet count and stiffen the run.
Filling bin 1, the first heap
A hot heap wants to be filled all at once if possible. Build the heap in 50 to 100 mm layers, alternating
browns and greens

by volume. Roughly two parts browns to one part greens, autumn leaves, shredded cardboard, straw, woodchip on the brown side; kitchen scraps, grass clippings, fresh prunings, comfrey leaves on the green side. The target
C:N ratio
is 25:1 to 30:1; the two-to-one volume rule gets you there in practice without weighing anything.

Moisture matters. Each layer should be wrung-out-sponge wet, squeeze a handful, one or two drops come out, the material doesn't crumble dry. Dry browns (autumn leaves stored in sacks) need wetting as you layer them in; very wet greens (a barrowload of grass clippings) need offsetting browns and a stir. Fill the bin to within 100 mm of the top, cover with cardboard or hessian, and wait. The first heat reading comes after 48 to 3 days.
The first temperature check

Push the compost thermometer probe into the centre of the heap, all the way to the dial. A well-built hot pile reads 50°C by day 2, 55 to 65°C by day 5, and stays above 50°C for around a week before falling to 40 to 45°C by day 14. Anything in this range is correct; the heap is working.

If the reading after 3 days is below 40°C, the heap has not started. The cause is almost always too few greens, too dry, or too small. The fix is to dismantle the heap into the next bin, rebuild with an additional layer of fresh greens and water as you go, and re-cover. The second build usually heats.
After the build, the cycle

The full cycle, from full bin 1 to harvested finished compost in bin 3, takes 8 to 12 weeks in summer and 12 to 16 weeks across winter (cooler ambient slows the second heat cycle). The schedule registered against this tutorial fires reminders on the right days; the short version:
Day 0: build the heap in bin 1.
Day 14: turn from bin 1 to bin 2. Outer cooler layers go to the centre.
Day 28: turn from bin 2 to bin 3. Material is dark and smells of forest floor.
Day 56 to 84: harvest finished compost from bin 3. Sieve or apply unsifted. Bin 1 is empty and ready for the next batch of fresh inputs.
What can go wrong

attrs.intro: Five things that go wrong on first hot heaps and the fix for each.

items.fix: Dismantle into the next bin. Rebuild adding 30 to 40% by volume of fresh greens (grass clippings, weeds, kitchen scraps). Wet each layer to wrung-out-sponge moisture as you go. Re-cover. Check at 3 days; the second build should heat.
items.cause: Too few greens (C:N too high), too dry, or the heap is below the 1 m³ critical mass.

items.fix: Turn the heap into the next bin, layering in dry browns (shredded cardboard, straw, autumn leaves) at one-to-one with the existing material. The smell clears within 24 hours of the rebuild as the heap aerates.
items.cause: Too many greens (C:N too low), insufficient air, or both. Anaerobic decomposition.
items.symptom: Heap smells of ammonia, sulphur, or rotting fish

items.fix: This is leachate. Add a layer of dry browns to the top of the heap. Check the cover is in place and rain-shedding. Catch the leachate in a tray for a few days and dilute 1:10 with water for use as a liquid feed.
items.cause: Too much moisture, wet greens added in a layer that's now compacted, or rainwater overflowing the cardboard cover.
items.symptom: Brown liquid pours from the base whenever you walk past

items.fix: Every kitchen addition should be immediately covered with a 50 to 100 mm layer of dry browns. Keep a sack of shredded cardboard or autumn leaves next to the bin for the purpose. The flies disperse within 48 hours of the cover going on.
items.cause: Exposed kitchen scraps on the top of the heap, not yet covered with browns.
items.symptom: Heap is full of fruit flies or wasps

items.fix: Sieve the finished compost through a 10 mm garden riddle. The fine compost is ready to use; the woody and shell fraction goes back into bin 1 as the next heap is built. The second pass through the cycle finishes most of it.
items.cause: Normal. Woody stems and eggshells need a second pass.
items.symptom: Heap is finished but full of un-rotted woody stems and eggshells

attrs.heading: Common compost-system problems

content.text: Care and maintenance

The pallet bins last 5 to 7 years before the bottom boards rot through. When a board rots, lift the bin off the soil with two helpers, knock out the rotten pallet, and slide in a replacement. The corners take the most weather; check them annually after the autumn turn and re-drive any loose screws.
The compost thermometer probe rusts at the gland over time. Wipe dry after each reading; replace at five years.
